_note: Self-study practice for Python Strings & Arithmetic. Inspired by Python Classroom — not an official platform.
title: LT Review

problems.title: Text Inspector
problems.description:
You are building a simple text inspection tool. A user types in a line of text and your program reports several facts about it using **string methods and slicing**.

Your program should:
1. Read a **single line of text** from the user.
2. Print the **total number of characters** (including spaces) using `len()`.
3. Print the text converted to **uppercase** using `upper()`.
4. Print the text converted to **lowercase** using `lower()`.
5. Print the text **reversed** using slicing.
constraints: The input can contain letters, digits, and spaces.
The input will be at least 1 character long.
problems.inputFormat: A single line of text (may contain letters, numbers, and spaces).
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Length: <n>` — total number of characters.
Line 2: `Upper: <text>` — the text in all uppercase.
Line 3: `Lower: <text>` — the text in all lowercase.
Line 4: `Reversed: <text>` — the text reversed.
problems.hint:
text = input()
print('Length:', len(text))
print('Upper:', text.upper())
print('Lower:', text.lower())
print('Reversed:', text[::-1])

problems.title: Number Statistics Calculator
problems.description:
You are writing a batch statistics tool. A user provides a count and then a series of numbers, and your program calculates key statistics using Python's **built-in functions**.

Your program should:
1. Read an integer **n** — the count of numbers to process.
2. Read **n integers** separated by spaces on the next line.
3. Calculate and display the **sum**, **minimum**, **maximum**, and **average** (rounded to 2 decimal places).
constraints: Average is rounded to 2 decimal places.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: An integer `n` — how many numbers to read.
Line 2: `n` integers separated by spaces.
problems.hint:
n = int(input())
nums = list(map(int, input().split()))
print('Sum:', sum(nums))
print('Min:', min(nums))
print('Max:', max(nums))
print('Average:', round(sum(nums)/len(nums), 2))

testCases.label: Hidden: Mix of negatives and positives

problems.title: Power and Remainder Calculator
problems.description:
You are building an arithmetic calculator that performs **power and division operations** on two numbers. The program must also gracefully handle division by zero.

Your program should:
1. Read two integers **a** (base) and **b** (exponent/divisor) from a single line.
2. Calculate and display:
   - **a raised to the power of b** using `**`
   - **Floor division** of a by b using `//`
   - **Remainder** of a divided by b using `%`
   - **Quotient and remainder together** using `divmod()`
3. If **b is 0**, print `Error: Cannot divide by zero` for the last three operations (but still compute the power).
constraints: Both a and b are integers.
a can be any integer; b can be zero or positive.
If b is 0, only the power result is valid — display the error message for division operations.
problems.inputFormat: A single line containing two integers `a` and `b` separated by a space.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Power: <result>` — a raised to b.
Line 2: `Floor Division: <result>` — or `Error: Cannot divide by zero`.
Line 3: `Remainder: <result>` — or `Error: Cannot divide by zero`.
Line 4: `Divmod: <result>` — or `Error: Cannot divide by zero`.
problems.hint:
a, b = map(int, input().split())
print('Power:', a**b)
if b == 0:
    print('Floor Division: Error: Cannot divide by zero')
    print('Remainder: Error: Cannot divide by zero')
    print('Divmod: Error: Cannot divide by zero')
else:
    print('Floor Division:', a//b)
    print('Remainder:', a%b)
    print('Divmod:', divmod(a,b))

testCases.label: Example 3 — b is zero
expected: Floor Division: Error: Cannot divide by zero
Remainder: Error: Cannot divide by zero
Divmod: Error: Cannot divide by zero

problems.title: [new] String Method Toolkit
problems.description:
You are building a string inspection utility that demonstrates a wide range of **Python string methods**.

Your program should:
1. Read a single line of text.
2. Read a **search word** to locate in the text.
3. Print the result of `find()` for the search word (returns -1 if not found).
4. Print how many times the search word appears using `count()`.
5. Read a **replacement word** and print the text after `replace()`-ing the search word with it.
6. Print whether the original text `startswith()` a prefix read from input.
7. Print whether the original text `endswith()` a suffix read from input.
8. Print `isalpha()` on the **search word**.
9. Read an integer **width** and print the search word padded with `zfill()`.
10. Print `swapcase()` of the original text.
constraints: The text may contain letters, digits, and spaces.
The search word is a single word (no spaces).
Width for zfill is between 1 and 10.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: The text string.
Line 2: The search word.
Line 3: The replacement word.
Line 4: A prefix string to check with startswith.
Line 5: A suffix string to check with endswith.
Line 6: An integer width for zfill.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Find: <n>`
Line 2: `Count: <n>`
Line 3: `Replaced: <text>`
Line 4: `Starts with '<prefix>': <True/False>`
Line 5: `Ends with '<suffix>': <True/False>`
Line 6: `Is alpha: <True/False>`
Line 7: `Zfill: <result>`
Line 8: `Swapcase: <text>`
problems.hint:
text = input()
search = input()
replace_word = input()
prefix = input()
suffix = input()
width = int(input())
print('Find:', text.find(search))
print('Count:', text.count(search))
print('Replaced:', text.replace(search, replace_word))
print(f"Starts with '{prefix}':", text.startswith(prefix))
print(f"Ends with '{suffix}':", text.endswith(suffix))
print('Is alpha:', search.isalpha())
print('Zfill:', search.zfill(width))
print('Swapcase:', text.swapcase())

testCases.label: Example 2 — word not found, digit search
Hidden: Search word with digits (isalpha false)

problems.title: [new] Formatted Report Printer
problems.description:
You are writing a formatted report printer that practices **all string formatting strategies**: f-strings with format specifiers, `%` operator, `.format()`, and Template strings.

Your program should:
1. Read a **student name**, an **integer score**, and a **float GPA** on separate lines.
2. Compute the **percentage** (score is out of 120) as a float.
3. Using an **f-string**: print the name right-aligned in a field of width 15, score zero-padded to width 5, and GPA to 2 decimal places.
4. Using the **% operator**: print `'Student: <name>, Score: <score>, GPA: <gpa>'` where name is `%s`, score is `%d`, GPA is `%.2f`.
5. Using **`.format()`**: print the percentage formatted to 1 decimal place followed by `%`.
6. Using a **Template string**: print `'Hello, <name>! Your GPA is <gpa>.'` substituting name and gpa (gpa formatted to 2 decimal places before substituting).
7. Print the GPA formatted with **center alignment** in a field of width 10 using an f-string.
8. Print the score formatted as **binary** using `format()`.
constraints: Name is a single word, no spaces.
Score is an integer between 0 and 120.
GPA is a float with up to 2 decimal places.
problems.inputFormat:
Line 1: Student name.
Line 2: Integer score (out of 120).
Line 3: Float GPA.
problems.outputFormat:
Line 1: `Name: <right-aligned name in 15 chars>, Score: <zero-padded 5-digit score>, GPA: <2 decimal gpa>`
Line 2: `Student: <name>, Score: <score>, GPA: <2 decimal gpa>` (using % operator)
Line 3: `Percentage: <1 decimal>%` (using .format())
Line 4: `Hello, <name>! Your GPA is <2 decimal gpa>.` (using Template)
Line 5: `GPA centered: |<gpa centered in 10 chars>|`
Line 6: `Score binary: <binary>`
problems.hint:
from string import Template
name = input()
score = int(input())
gpa = float(input())
pct = score / 120 * 100
print(f'Name: {name:>15}, Score: {score:05d}, GPA: {gpa:.2f}')
print('Student: %s, Score: %d, GPA: %.2f' % (name, score, gpa))
print('Percentage: {:.1f}%'.format(pct))
t = Template('Hello, $name! Your GPA is $gpa.')
print(t.substitute(name=name, gpa=f'{gpa:.2f}'))
print(f'GPA centered: |{gpa:^10.2f}|')
print('Score binary:', format(score, 'b'))
